title: Cat Eye Bulging and Red: Emergency Care Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has a bulging, red eye, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about causes, triage, and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: A bulging and red eye in a cat is a critical veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to prevent permanent blindness or the loss of the eye.

What causes a bulging and red eye in cats?

  • A bulging eye, medically known as exophthalmos or proptosis, occurs when the eye is pushed forward from its normal position.
  • Redness typically indicates significant inflammation, increased intraocular pressure, or a physical blockage behind the globe.
  • Common causes include glaucoma, abscesses behind the eye, severe trauma, or orbital tumors.
  • This condition reflects a serious underlying medical crisis rather than just a cosmetic issue.

Is a bulging red eye a veterinary emergency for my cat?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority emergency.
  • A bulging and red eye is extremely painful and carries a high risk of permanent blindness if not treated within hours.
  • Do not wait for a scheduled appointment; contact the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately to prevent irreversible damage and manage your cat's pain.

How can a photo of my cat's eye help the veterinary triage team?

  • A clear, well-lit photograph allows a veterinarian to remotely assess the severity of the protrusion and the condition of the cornea.
  • Photos help identify the presence of any discharge or hemorrhage before you arrive at the clinic.
  • This information allows the team to prepare necessary specialists and equipment so they are ready the moment you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Traumatic proptosis, the luxation of the globe from the orbit, often follows blunt trauma. Secondary orbital hemorrhage and swelling can further displace the globe. Clinical signs include a congested and red conjunctiva. In cats, proptosis usually results from severe head trauma, potentially involving other facial bone fractures. Immediate treatment involves providing moisture to the exposed corneoconjunctiva and replacing the globe under general anesthesia, if the animal's physical condition permits. Treatment includes systemic and topical antibiotics, and occasionally corticosteroids with topical mydriatics. The prognosis for return of vision is poor in cats. Anterior uveitis, another cause of red eye, presents clinically as photophobia, pain, blepharospasm, a congested and red conjunctiva, and corneal edema.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1696)

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Eye conditions involving bulging are considered time-sensitive emergencies. Waiting even a few hours can lead to the rupture of the eye or permanent vision loss.

faqs.question: How can I help my cat on the way to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure carrier to minimize movement. If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), put it on them to prevent them from pawing at or further damaging the eye.

faqs.question: Is a bulging eye always painful for a cat?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even if your cat is acting stoic, the pressure and inflammation associated with a bulging eye cause significant distress and pain.
